meta_title: For Students: analytics setup to Solve no SaaS knowledge (Beginner Tutorial)
meta_description: Learn how to analytics setup and overcome no SaaS knowledge. A Beginner-level Tutorial using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: Analytics Setup for Students: Solve No SaaS Knowledge
content.seo_description: Master analytics setup to overcome no SaaS knowledge. A beginner-friendly tutorial using Antigravity and Claude Code to launch your first real-world app.
content.answer_block: To solve the 'no SaaS knowledge' barrier, implement lightweight event tracking immediately. By integrating telemetry into your MVP, you transform raw traffic into actionable data. Use Antigravity for rapid scaffolding and Claude Code to automate implementation, ensuring your first project provides the metrics needed to iterate and prove product-market fit.
content.intro:
The blank screen of a new SaaS project is intimidating, especially when you lack the roadmap to turn code into a business. Most students stall here, paralyzed by the fear that their first application will launch into a vacuum of silence, leaving them with no feedback and no growth. You are not just writing code; you are building a digital lighthouse.

By implementing analytics from day one, you shift from guessing to observing. This tutorial strips away the complexity of enterprise-grade tracking, providing you with a streamlined, professional-grade setup. With Antigravity and Claude Code as your co-pilots, you will bridge the gap between a student project and a data-driven SaaS product.

steps.title: Define Your North Star Metric
steps.description: Before writing code, identify the single most important action a user takes in your app. This prevents data bloat and keeps your focus on user value.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze the core user flow of my SaaS project. Suggest the top 3 events I should track to measure success, keeping it beginner-friendly and actionable.
steps.claude_code_command: claude ask 'Analyze my current project files and list the critical user actions that represent value realization.'

steps.title: Initialize the Analytics Provider
steps.description: Choose a lightweight provider like PostHog or Umami. We will use a wrapper to keep the implementation modular and easy to swap later.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a clean, reusable Analytics wrapper class in TypeScript that can handle event tracking and user identification.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Create a new file src/utils/analytics.ts and implement a singleton wrapper for event tracking.'

steps.title: Inject Telemetry into Components
steps.description: Strategically place tracking calls in your UI components. Focus on buttons, form submissions, and page views to capture the user journey.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Show me how to wrap my primary CTA button component with an analytics event trigger without bloating the existing logic.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Find the primary CTA button in the codebase and inject the analytics.track() method on click.'

steps.title: Automate User Context Enrichment
steps.description: Don't just track events; track who triggered them. Identify users to see the actual path of individual customers through your app.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a middleware function that automatically attaches user metadata to every analytics event sent from the client.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Create a middleware that extracts user identity from the auth context and attaches it to analytics events.'

steps.title: Verify Data Flow and Debug
steps.description: Use a development mode to ensure your events are firing correctly before you deploy to production.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a simple test script or log-based verification system to ensure my analytics events are firing with the correct payload.
steps.claude_code_command: claude ask 'How can I verify that my analytics events are firing correctly in the browser console during development?'

pro_tips: Track 'failed' attempts; users struggling to sign up is more valuable data than users succeeding.
Never send PII (Personally Identifiable Information) like emails or passwords to your analytics provider.
Keep your event names in a centralized constant file to avoid 'string-typo' bugs that ruin your data integrity.
Use session recording in moderation; it is the ultimate tool for seeing where your UI confuses new users.
Set up a 'Data Health' dashboard early so you know immediately if your tracking script stops working.
content.next_steps: Now that your tracking is live, deploy your app and wait for the first 10 users. Spend 30 minutes each day reviewing the event stream to identify the exact point where users drop off, then iterate your UI based on that specific bottleneck.
content.conversion_block: You have the tools, the strategy, and the technical edge to transform your idea into a measurable success. Stop building in the dark and start engineering for growth. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
